h1: Georgia Property Tax Abatement Request Letter Generator
metaTitle: Georgia Property Tax Abatement Demand Letter | Appeal Tool
metaDescription: Generate a Georgia property tax abatement request letter. Challenge your assessment under O.C.G.A. § 48-5-311 within 45 days. Free, fast, state-specific.
deadline: 45 days from the date of the assessment notice
penalty: Refund of overpaid taxes plus interest at the prime rate, and frozen assessment value for two years if appeal succeeds
introParagraph: If you own property in Georgia and believe your county tax assessor has overvalued your home, land, or commercial building, you have a legal right to appeal. Georgia law gives property owners just 45 days from the date the Annual Notice of Assessment is mailed to file a written appeal with the County Board of Tax Assessors. Missing this deadline usually means waiting another full year. A well-drafted abatement request letter is the first formal step: it documents your dispute, identifies the grounds for reduction, and triggers the county's obligation to review or forward your case to the Board of Equalization. Acting quickly and in writing protects your rights and your wallet.
lawExplanation: Georgia's property tax appeal system is governed primarily by O.C.G.A. § 48-5-311, which establishes the County Board of Tax Assessors, the Board of Equalization, and the procedures for challenging an assessment. Under Georgia law, all real property must be assessed at 40% of its fair market value as of January 1 of the tax year. Each year, counties mail an Annual Notice of Assessment showing the current value, prior year value, and estimated taxes. Property owners may appeal on four grounds: (1) value (the assessor overestimated fair market value), (2) uniformity (your property is assessed higher than comparable properties), (3) taxability (the property is exempt or partially exempt), or (4) denial of homestead exemption. Appeals must be filed within 45 days of the notice date. Once filed, the Board of Tax Assessors has the option to adjust the value. If they decline or the owner rejects their adjustment, the case proceeds to the County Board of Equalization, a hearing officer (for non-homestead property valued over $500,000), or binding arbitration. Either party may then appeal the decision to the Superior Court within 30 days. A significant benefit under O.C.G.A. § 48-5-299(c) is the 'freeze' provision: if the appeal results in a reduced value, that value generally cannot be increased for the next two tax years, except for substantial physical changes. Owners who pay disputed taxes and prevail are entitled to a refund with interest. Georgia also recognizes special programs like Conservation Use Valuation and Forest Land Protection that may reduce taxable value substantially.
letterStrategy: An effective Georgia property tax abatement letter should be sent to the County Board of Tax Assessors at the address listed on your Annual Notice of Assessment, by certified mail with return receipt requested, well before the 45-day deadline. The letter should clearly identify the property by parcel ID, owner name, and address, then state which of the four statutory grounds you are appealing on—value, uniformity, taxability, or denial of exemption. Include your opinion of the property's fair market value and the evidence supporting it: recent comparable sales (within the prior 12 months), a recent appraisal, photographs of damage or deferred maintenance, contractor repair estimates, income and expense statements for rental property, or assessments of nearby comparable properties showing inconsistent valuations. Specify which appeal route you choose: Board of Equalization (free), hearing officer (for high-value commercial or non-homestead property), or binding arbitration (requires a certified appraisal). A demand letter signals you understand your rights under O.C.G.A. § 48-5-311 and are prepared to escalate. Many counties offer informal review during the 45-day window, and a clear, well-documented letter often results in a no-change settlement letter offering reduction, which you can accept within 30 days to avoid a hearing. If you reject the offer or receive no response within 180 days, your appeal automatically advances. Always keep copies and proof of mailing.
proceduralNotes: Filing an appeal with the County Board of Tax Assessors is free. Hearings before the Board of Equalization are also free and typically scheduled within 90-180 days. Hearing officer appeals require the property to be non-homestead and valued over $500,000. Binding arbitration requires the taxpayer to submit a certified appraisal within 45 days and pay arbitrator costs if the final value is closer to the county's number. Superior Court appeals must be filed within 30 days of the BOE decision and carry standard civil filing fees, generally $200-$220, varying by county. Georgia's small claims (Magistrate Court) limit is $15,000, but tax assessment appeals are not heard there—they follow the statutory administrative path. You must continue paying taxes during appeal to avoid penalties.

faq.q: How long do I have to appeal my Georgia property tax assessment?
faq.a: You have exactly 45 days from the date printed on your Annual Notice of Assessment to file a written appeal with the County Board of Tax Assessors. This is a strict deadline under O.C.G.A. § 48-5-311. If you miss it, you generally must wait until the following tax year to challenge the value. Mail your appeal by certified mail with return receipt or hand-deliver it to ensure proof of timely filing. Email or online portal submissions are accepted in many counties—check your specific county's procedure.

faq.q: What grounds can I use to appeal my property tax assessment in Georgia?
faq.a: Georgia recognizes four grounds: value (the assessor's fair market value is too high), uniformity (your property is taxed higher than comparable nearby properties), taxability (the property qualifies for an exemption), and denial of homestead or other exemption. Most appeals are based on value or uniformity. Strong evidence includes recent comparable sales, a private appraisal, photos of property defects, contractor repair estimates, or assessment data showing similar homes valued lower. The burden initially rests on you to show the county's value is incorrect.

faq.q: Will my property taxes be frozen if I win my appeal?
faq.a: Yes. Under O.C.G.A. § 48-5-299(c), if your appeal results in a reduced fair market value, that reduced value is generally locked in for the next two successive tax years. The county cannot raise it during that period unless substantial physical changes occur, such as new construction, additions, or major renovations. This 'freeze' is one of the strongest reasons to appeal even when the immediate tax savings seem modest—the cumulative three-year benefit can be significant for homeowners and commercial owners alike.

faq.q: Do I have to keep paying my property taxes while my appeal is pending?
faq.a: Yes. You must pay at least the temporary tax bill, which is typically based on 85% of the current assessment (or 100% of the prior year's value if no prior appeal). Failing to pay can result in interest, penalties, and tax liens. If you win your appeal and the value is reduced, the county will issue a refund with interest at the prime rate for any overpayment. Continuing to pay protects your credit, your property, and preserves your refund rights.

faq.q: What happens after I send my abatement request letter?
faq.a: The Board of Tax Assessors must review your appeal and respond, typically within 180 days. They may issue a no-change letter (sending the case directly to the Board of Equalization), or offer a reduced value in a 30-day option letter. You can accept the offer in writing within 30 days, or reject it to proceed to a hearing. If the county fails to respond within 180 days, your appeal is automatically forwarded to the Board of Equalization for a hearing.
